behaviors.description: Required patient fields (name, DOB, insurance ID) are validated and missing items produce a clear error listing all missing fields
behaviors.justification: Pure synchronous input validation logic with no I/O or dependencies. A mock can't miss this—validation is self-contained. Field checking is deterministic.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: parametrized inputs with various missing/present field combinations. Verify the error message lists exactly the missing items (not fewer, not more) and is human-readable.

behaviors.description: Date of birth validation rejects future dates, dates >150 years ago, and non-date strings
behaviors.justification: Fixed business rules with no external dependencies. Pure date validation logic is deterministic and synchronous.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: parametrized test with future dates, dates from year 1800s, non-parseable strings, boundary cases (today, exactly 150 years ago), and valid dates. Verify rejection/acceptance on each.

behaviors.description: Insurance eligibility check sends correct patient ID and plan code to payer API and correctly interprets eligible/not eligible/pending response
behaviors.justification: Calls external API. While response interpretation could be tested with a mock at Unit level, real-world failures occur at the HTTP boundary: serialization errors, schema mismatches, authentication failures, or API changes. Insurance data is too critical to rely on mocks. REAL-INFRA applies—real API or HTTP mock server is needed to catch request format issues.
behaviors.test_description: System/Integration test: use HTTP mock server (e.g., wiremock, responses library) or real test payer API endpoint. Verify: (a) request body contains correct patient ID and plan code in expected JSON/XML format, (b) each response type (eligible/not eligible/pending) is correctly parsed and applied to the patient record. Include at least one request capture to inspect headers and body.

behaviors.description: When payer API is unreachable, system marks eligibility as pending and continues intake instead of blocking
behaviors.justification: Tests error handling path. A simple mocked exception (Unit level) would verify the code *handles* an exception, but won't test real network failure modes: timeouts, connection refused, DNS failures, half-open connections. REAL-INFRA applies—real network behavior must be simulated to catch edge cases.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: mock HTTP layer to simulate connection timeout, refused connection, or DNS failure. Verify: (a) intake process does NOT raise/propagate the error, (b) eligibility field is set to 'pending', (c) subsequent steps (triage, scheduling) proceed with 'pending' status, (d) patient record is still created/stored.

behaviors.description: Symptom triage correctly assigns high urgency to emergency symptom combinations (e.g., chest pain + shortness of breath)
behaviors.justification: Correctness is entirely dependent on the real LLM's medical reasoning. LLM-DEP applies strongly here. Mocking the LLM to return 'high urgency' would hide whether the LLM *actually* recognizes emergency patterns. You can't verify medical correctness without the real model.
behaviors.test_description: Agentic test: call the real medical triage LLM with multiple symptom combinations. Include: (a) clear emergency cases (chest pain + SOB, severe abdominal pain + vomiting), (b) non-emergency cases (mild cough, headache), (c) borderline cases. Verify high urgency returned for emergencies, lower for non-emergencies. Run multiple times or with temperature sampling to account for LLM non-determinism.

behaviors.description: Symptom triage produces a natural language explanation of urgency assessment, not just a numeric score, suitable for clinician review
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP strongly applies. The explanation must be generated by the real LLM to be clinically valid and coherent. Mocking would hide whether the explanation is actually understandable or medically sound. A mock returning 'good explanation' tells you nothing about real output.
behaviors.test_description: Agentic test: submit real symptom data to the LLM. Verify response structure includes: (a) urgency score/level, (b) natural language explanation. Check explanation mentions reported symptoms and uses clinical reasoning, not generic language. Spot-check 3–5 real outputs for clinician readability.

behaviors.description: Appointment scheduled in first available slot matching urgency level (high = same-day, medium = within 48h)
behaviors.justification: Tests scheduling algorithm + database interaction. MOCK-MISS applies: a pure mock scheduler won't catch schema mismatches, constraint violations, or time-calculation errors in the real database. Must interact with real calendar data to verify the algorithm correctly filters by urgency and slot availability.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with real database (or strong mock with transaction support). Prepopulate calendar with known appointments. Submit 2–3 test patients with different urgency levels (high, medium, low) and verify each is assigned to the first available slot that respects their urgency constraint. Verify timestamps are correct (same-day for high, within 48h for medium).

behaviors.description: Appointment time slot is not double-booked when two patients are processed concurrently
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA + concurrency boundary. MOCK-MISS strongly applies: simple mocks won't catch race conditions in transaction isolation, row locking, or timing windows. Only real concurrent execution against the actual database can reveal whether the booking is protected by proper locking or transactions.
behaviors.test_description: System test with real database. Spawn two concurrent patient intake processes (actual threads or processes, not sequential). Both target the same high-urgency slot (e.g., same doctor, same time window). Use database connection introspection or transaction logs to confirm: (a) only one patient's record is committed with that appointment, (b) the second either gets a different slot or receives a booking failure. Repeat 5–10 times to catch intermittent race conditions.

behaviors.description: Confirmation message includes patient name, appointment time, provider name, and preparation instructions appropriate to visit type
behaviors.justification: Tests message generation and data aggregation. Could be Unit-tested with mocked lookups, but MOCK-MISS applies: mocks might return dummy data while real database lookups fail or return incomplete records. Integration level ensures the message pulls all fields from real (or realistic mock) data.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: execute a complete intake (with real DB, mocked LLM/insurance OK) and capture the confirmation message. Verify message contains: (a) correct patient name, (b) scheduled appointment date/time, (c) assigned provider name, (d) preparation instructions that match the visit type (e.g., fasting for blood work, bring insurance card). Check formatting is clear and professional.

behaviors.description: Full intake pipeline (validate → check insurance → triage → schedule → confirm) completes for a real patient submission without manual intervention
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP + LLM-DEP + REAL-INFRA all apply. The pipeline has multiple agentic steps (LLM triage), real I/O (insurance API, database writes), and sequence dependencies. A single failed step breaks the whole flow. Only end-to-end testing with real external services can catch integration failures.
behaviors.test_description: Workflow test: submit a complete patient intake with real/realistic data (name, DOB, insurance ID, symptom history). System must: (a) validate all fields, (b) call real insurance API (or mock), (c) call real LLM for triage, (d) write appointment to real DB, (e) generate and send confirmation message. Verify all steps complete and patient record is fully populated. Test with 2–3 patient scenarios to catch step interdependencies.
